text:
{
 "prose": "Elias packs his instruments away one by one, without a word, the sextant and the sounding lead and the chart cases that brought him ashore in the first place, and Maren watches him do it from the gallery doorway and says nothing either.

There is nothing to say that would not make it worse. The lie has settled into the lighthouse's small rooms like weather of its own, riding between them at breakfast, at the evening trim, in the narrow space they cross a dozen times a day on the stair. It does not shout. It does not need to. It simply waits, patient as the thing below the stilts, taking up a little more room every week that neither of them speaks of it.

The storm season wears itself out at last into long gray days and longer gray evenings. Word comes up from the harbor master by the weekly boat: the survey ship that dropped Elias on this rock is due back on the tide, come to collect him and whatever soundings he managed to salvage from a coastline that would not hold still for him.

Maren finds she is not ready for the sound of that, though she has had weeks to prepare for it. She goes on trimming the wick at the hours the light demands, watching Elias fold his cases shut with the same careful hands he once used to unpack them, and waits, with him, for the ship that is coming to take the question of him away from her rock, one way or another.
",
 "micro_details": []
}
